full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding JSON file structure for Part B
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: I'm going through my Part B LLM logs and making sure each task's JSON is correct. I have separate files for each task—some have only 2 or 3 interactions. Does every file still need its own student_metadata and student_declaration, and for top_5_prompts do I list all of them even when there are fewer than 5?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude confirmed each file must be self-contained with its own metadata and declaration. For top_5_prompts, it said to list every interaction I have when there are fewer than five. I didn't know that before—I had left top_5 empty on the tasks where I'd only used the LLM a couple of times. I went back and filled those in.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I re-read the Part B instructions and found the line about 'if fewer than five interactions were logged for a task, include all of them.' That matched what Claude said. I updated my llm_task_2_3 and llm_task_4_2 files so the top_5_prompts list every interaction I actually had.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Clarifying when to mark code as verbatim
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: For code_used_verbatim: I asked Claude how to structure the FGM cutting-plane loop (like what to return from run_fgm and how to track the working set). I didn't copy any code—I wrote it myself in Python following that idea. Should that be true or false? I want to report it correctly.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude said verbatim means the exact code was copied without change. If I got structural or conceptual help but wrote the implementation myself, that's false. I was leaning that way already but wanted to be sure so I'm consistent across all my task JSONs.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I went through each interaction where I had coding help. Where I'd pasted a snippet and then edited it, I left code_used_verbatim as true or added a note. Where I'd only asked 'how should I structure X' and then coded it myself, I set it to false and described that in how_it_helped.

full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: The instructions say student_verification has to be in my own words and show I checked the response. What makes a verification good—should I be citing section numbers from the paper or is it enough to say I re-read the instructions and it matched?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude suggested tying each verification to something concrete—e.g. a paper section, an equation, or something I actually did (like 'I re-ran the notebook and got X'). I used that as a guide and went back over my logs. For the ones where the LLM had cited the paper, I added the section or equation so the verification is traceable.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I applied this to all my interaction entries. For example in Task 2.3, my verification for the 'result gap' prompt now references Table 1 (dataset), Section 2.3 (MKL), Section 3.4 (30 runs), and B from 3.4—so an examiner can see I didn't just accept the answer but checked it against the paper.

top_5_prompts.prompt: I'm going through my Part B LLM logs and making sure each task's JSON is correct. I have separate files for each task—some have only 2 or 3 interactions. Does every file still need its own student_metadata and student_declaration, and for top_5_prompts do I list all of them even when there are fewer than 5?
top_5_prompts.why_important: I wasn't sure if files with fewer interactions still needed to be self-contained. Confirmed I should list every interaction in top_5_prompts when there are fewer than five, so I went back and filled those in.

top_5_prompts.prompt: For code_used_verbatim: I asked Claude how to structure the FGM cutting-plane loop (like what to return from run_fgm and how to track the working set). I didn't copy any code—I wrote it myself in Python following that idea. Should that be true or false? I want to report it correctly.
top_5_prompts.why_important: I wanted to be honest about what counted as verbatim. Once I knew it meant exact copy, I could consistently mark my 'structure only' help as false across all tasks.

top_5_prompts.prompt: The instructions say student_verification has to be in my own words and show I checked the response. What makes a verification good—should I be citing section numbers from the paper or is it enough to say I re-read the instructions and it matched?
top_5_prompts.why_important: I wanted my verifications to actually show I'd checked the LLM's answers. Using concrete references (sections, equations) made the logs more credible and helped me double-check my own work.

student_declaration.statement: I declare that this JSON file contains a complete and honest record of my LLM usage for Task 4.2. I used the LLM to clarify the disclosure format and how to report my usage accurately; I then verified each point against the Part B instructions and my own notebooks.
